core_concepts: Range of Motion (ROM) & Hypertrophy: Greater ROM, particularly in the stretched position, is a primary driver of muscle growth.
Mechanical Tension: The primary stimulus for hypertrophy; must be applied through a full range of motion.
Partial vs. Full ROM: Full ROM is generally superior for development, but partials at the long end (stretch) can be effective when added to full ROM work.
Joint Health and Stability: ROM must be balanced against joint integrity; if a position causes pain, it is not a high-stimulus exercise for that individual.
Individual Anatomy: Anatomical differences (e.g., limb length, hip structure) dictate what constitutes a 'full' ROM for a specific person.

actionable_advice.full_rom_priority: Prioritize exercises that allow for a complete stretch of the target muscle (e.g., deep squats, incline dumbbell curls).
actionable_advice.the_stretch_rule: Spend more time in the lengthened (stretched) phase of the lift, using a controlled eccentric (2-3 seconds).
actionable_advice.partial_inclusion: If training past technical failure, add partials at the long end of the ROM (e.g., partial rep leg extensions or flys), not the shortened end.
actionable_advice.monitoring: If full ROM causes pain or technical breakdown (e.g., lumbar rounding in a deadlift), truncate the ROM slightly to stay within a safe, effective zone.
actionable_advice.consistency: Track ROM metrics (e.g., bar depth, degree of knee flexion) with as much rigor as load or reps.

stretch_mediated_hypertrophy.description: Training at longer muscle lengths produces significantly greater hypertrophic responses compared to shorter lengths.

rom_generalization.description: Full ROM is generally superior to partial ROM for hypertrophy across most compound movements.

partial_reps_utility.description: Partials in the stretched position can be effective, whereas partials in the shortened position are generally less effective for hypertrophy.

coaching_insights: Don't compromise full ROM just to increase the load; a lighter weight with a deep stretch is often more hypertrophic than a heavy ego-lift.
A client's 'full ROM' is individual; stop comparing depth to others and focus on their specific biomechanical limit.
If a movement pattern prevents a muscle from reaching its fully stretched position (e.g., lying leg curls), it is an inferior exercise choice regardless of loading potential.
Tempo is the key to ROM: A rushed eccentric phase is a wasted opportunity to accumulate mechanical tension in the lengthened position.
common_mistakes: Cutting ROM to hit higher weight loads (ego lifting).
Using partial reps at the 'top' (contracted) end of a movement instead of the 'bottom' (stretched) end.
Ignoring the eccentric phase, effectively bypassing the most hypertrophic part of the movement.
Assuming every exercise should be taken to the same ROM regardless of joint health or anatomy.
Ignoring technique breakdown in order to reach an arbitrary 'number' or rep count.

motivational_quotes: Master the depth, own the muscle.
Quality range is better than heavy weight.
The stretch is where growth lives.
Don't cheat your body out of the stimulus.
If you don't use the full range, you aren't using the full muscle.
Intensity is the goal; range is the medium.
Respect the anatomy, maximize the movement.
Consistency in range creates consistency in growth.
myths_addressed: Heavy weight is all that matters.
Full ROM is dangerous for everyone.
The 'pump' is more important than the stretch.
You don't need a deep stretch to grow.
Tempo doesn't matter as long as you move the weight.
If you're not in pain, you aren't doing full ROM.
You should always strive for the same ROM as your favorite bodybuilder.
Going deep on squats is bad for your knees.

grit_application.implementation_strategy: The app should include a 'Range of Motion' module. 1. For key lifts, provide 'Depth Benchmarks' using the device's camera for AI-based form verification. 2. 'Tempo Coaches' that force a specific eccentric count (e.g., 3s down) during high-priority hypertrophy blocks. 3. 'Stretch Bias' tagging in the exercise library, prioritizing movements that allow for deep lengthened-position loading. 4. 'ROM Logging' where users track depth markers for compound lifts.
